<a id="key-eas"></a>[Definition:]Let the **eligible item set**be the set consisting of every attribute or element information item for which **all**of the following are true:1 its [validation context] is the [·validation root·](#key-vr);2 <a id="anchor9923"></a> its [schema actual value] is not [·absent·](#key-null); 3 <a id="anchor10235"></a> its [·governing·](#key-governing) type definition is the built-in simple type definition for [ID](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#ID), [IDREF](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#IDREF), or [IDREFS](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#IDREFS), or a type definition [·derived·](#key-derived) or [·constructed·](#key-constructed) directly (in a single derivation step) or indirectly (through one or more steps) from any of these. **Note:**The use of [schema actual value] in the definition of [·eligible item set·](#key-eas) above means that ***default***or ***fixed***value constraints may play a part in the [·eligible item set·](#key-eas). **Note:**Element information items that have complex types with simple content can appear in the [·eligible item set·](#key-eas). <a id="anchor10662b"></a>Then there is one **ID/IDREF binding**in the [[ID/IDREF table]](#e-ii_table) for every distinct string which is an [·ID value·](#key-TYPE-value) or an [·IDREF value·](#key-TYPE-value) in the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of a member of the [·eligible item set·](#key-eas).Each **ID/IDREF binding**has properties as follows:PSVI Contributions forID/IDREF binding information items
**<a id="iib-id"></a>[id]**
: The string identified above.

**<a id="iib-binding"></a>[binding]**
: A set consisting of every element information item for which **all**of the following are true:1 its [[validation context]](#e-validation_context) is the [·validation root·](#key-vr);2 it has an attribute information item in its [[attributes]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) or an element information item in its [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) whose [·actual value·](#key-vv) is or contains the [[id]](#iib-id) of this **ID/IDREF binding**and the corresponding type definition is or is [·derived·](#key-derived) from [ID](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#ID).

The net effect of the above is to have one entry for every string used as an id, whether by declaration or by reference, associated with those elements, if any, which actually purport to have that id. See [Validation Root Valid (ID/IDREF) (§3.3.4.5)](#cvc-id) above for the validation rule which actually checks for errors here.**Note:**The **ID/IDREF binding**information item, unlike most other aspects of this specification, is essentially an internal bookkeeping mechanism. It is introduced to support the definition of [Validation Root Valid (ID/IDREF) (§3.3.4.5)](#cvc-id) above.
#### <a id="coss-schema"></a>3.17.6 Constraints on Schemas as a Whole

##### <a id="sec-sch-props-correct"></a>3.17.6.1 Schema Properties Correct

All schemas (see [Schemas as a Whole (§3.17)](#Schemas)) must satisfy the following constraint.

<a id="sch-props-correct"></a>**Schema Component Constraint: Schema Properties Correct**
**All**of the following must be true:1 The values of the properties of a schema are as described in the property tableau in [The Schema Itself (§3.17.1)](#Schema_details), modulo the impact of [Missing Sub-components (§5.3)](#conformance-missing);2<a id="c-nmd"></a>None of the [{type definitions}](#s-type_definitions), [{attribute declarations}](#s-attribute_declarations), [{element declarations}](#s-element_declarations), [{attribute group definitions}](#s-attribute_group_definitions), [{model group definitions}](#s-model_group_definitions), [{notation declarations}](#s-notation_declarations), or [{identity-constraint definitions}](#s-identity-constraint_definitions) properties contains two or more schema components with the same [expanded name](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname).
##### <a id="sec-src-resolve"></a>3.17.6.2 QName resolution (Schema Document)

<a id="src-resolve"></a>**Schema Representation Constraint: QName resolution (Schema Document)**
For a [·QName·](#gloss-QName) to resolve to a schema component of a specified kind **all**of the following must be true:1 That component is a member of the value of the appropriate property of the schema which corresponds to the schema document within which the [·QName·](#gloss-QName) appears, that is the appropriate **case**among the following is true:1.1 **If **the kind specified is simple or complex type definition, **then **the property is the [{type definitions}](#s-type_definitions).1.2 **If **the kind specified is attribute declaration, **then **the property is the [{attribute declarations}](#s-attribute_declarations).1.3 **If **the kind specified is element declaration, **then **the property is the [{element declarations}](#s-element_declarations).1.4 **If **the kind specified is attribute group, **then **the property is the [{attribute group definitions}](#s-attribute_group_definitions).1.5 **If **the kind specified is model group, **then **the property is the [{model group definitions}](#s-model_group_definitions).1.6 **If **the kind specified is notation declaration, **then **the property is the [{notation declarations}](#s-notation_declarations).1.7 **If **the kind specified is identity-constraint definition, **then **the property is the [{identity-constraint definitions}](#s-identity-constraint_definitions).2 The component's {name} matches the [·local name·](#q-local) of the [·QName·](#gloss-QName);3 The component's {target namespace} is identical to the [·namespace name·](#q-uri) of the [·QName·](#gloss-QName);4<a id="cl.qnr.nsdeclared"></a>The appropriate **case**among the following is true:4.1 **If **the [·namespace name·](#q-uri) of the [·QName·](#gloss-QName) is [·absent·](#key-null), **then ****one**of the following is true4.1.1 The [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item of the schema document containing the [·QName·](#gloss-QName) has no `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element).4.1.2 The [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item of the that schema document contains an [<import>](#element-import) element information item with no `namespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element).4.2 **otherwise **the [·namespace name·](#q-uri) of the [·QName·](#gloss-QName) is the same as **one**of the following:4.2.1 The [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of the [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item of the schema document containing the [·QName·](#gloss-QName).4.2.2 The [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `namespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of some [<import>](#element-import) element information item contained in the [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item of that schema document.4.2.3 `http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema`. 4.2.4 `http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance`.
##### <a id="sec-cvc-resolve-instance"></a>3.17.6.3 QName resolution (Instance)

As the discussion above at [Schema Component Details (§3)](#components) makes clear, at the level of schema components and [·validation·](#key-vn), reference to components by name is normally not involved. In a few cases, however, qualified names appearing in information items being [·validated·](#key-vn)must be resolved to schema components by such lookup. The following constraint is appealed to in these cases.

<a id="cvc-resolve-instance"></a>**Validation Rule: QName resolution (Instance)**
A pair of a local name and a namespace name (or [·absent·](#key-null)) resolve to a schema component of a specified kind in the context of [·validation·](#key-vn) by appeal to the appropriate property of the schema being used for the [·assessment·](#key-va). Each such property indexes components by name. The property to use is determined by the kind of component specified, that is, the appropriate **case**among the following must be true:1 **If **the kind specified is simple or complex type definition, **then **the property is the [{type definitions}](#s-type_definitions).2 **If **the kind specified is attribute declaration, **then **the property is the [{attribute declarations}](#s-attribute_declarations).3 **If **the kind specified is element declaration, **then **the property is the [{element declarations}](#s-element_declarations).4 **If **the kind specified is attribute group, **then **the property is the [{attribute group definitions}](#s-attribute_group_definitions).5 **If **the kind specified is model group, **then **the property is the [{model group definitions}](#s-model_group_definitions).6 **If **the kind specified is notation declaration, **then **the property is the [{notation declarations}](#s-notation_declarations). The component resolved to is the entry in the table whose {name} matches the local name of the pair and whose {target namespace} is identical to the namespace name of the pair.
## <a id="composition"></a>4 Schemas and Namespaces: Access and Composition

This chapter defines the mechanisms by which this specification establishes the necessary precondition for [·assessment·](#key-va), namely access to one or more schemas. This chapter also sets out in detail the relationship between schemas and namespaces, as well as mechanisms for modularization of schemas, including provision for incorporating definitions and declarations from one schema in another, possibly with modifications.

[Conformance (§2.4)](#concepts-conformance) describes three levels of conformance for schema processors, and [Schemas and Schema-validity Assessment (§5)](#conformance) provides a formal definition of [·assessment·](#key-va). This section sets out in detail the 3-layer architecture implied by the three conformance levels. The layers are:

1. The [·assessment·](#key-va) core, relating schema components and instance information items;
2. Schema representation: the connections between XML representations and schema components, including the relationships between namespaces and schema components;
3. XSD web-interoperability guidelines: connections from instance to schema (or schema document) and from schema document to schema (or schema document) for the WWW.
Layer 1 specifies the manner in which a schema composed of schema components can be applied to in the [·assessment·](#key-va) of an element information item. Layer 2 specifies the use of [<schema>](#element-schema) elements in XML documents as the standard XML representation for schema information in a broad range of computer systems and execution environments. To support interoperation over the World Wide Web in particular, layer 3 provides a set of conventions for schema reference on the Web. Additional details on each of the three layers are provided in the sections below.

### <a id="layer1"></a>4.1 Layer 1: Summary of the Schema-validity Assessment Core

The fundamental purpose of the [·assessment·](#key-va) core is to define [·assessment·](#key-va) for a single element information item and its descendants, with respect to a complex type definition. All processors are required to implement this core predicate in a manner which conforms exactly to this specification.

[·Assessment·](#key-va) is defined with reference to an [·XSD schema·](#key-schema) (note *not*a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)).

As specified above, each schema component is associated directly or indirectly with a target namespace, or explicitly with no namespace. In the case of multi-namespace documents, components for more than one target namespace will co-exist in a schema.

Processors have the option to assemble (and perhaps to optimize or pre-compile) the entire schema prior to the start of an [·assessment·](#key-va) episode, or to gather the schema lazily as individual components are required. In all cases it is required that:

- The processor succeed in locating the [·schema components·](#c) transitively required to complete an [·assessment·](#key-va) (note that components derived from [·schema documents·](#key-schemaDoc) can be integrated with components obtained through other means);
- no definition or declaration changes once it has been established;
- if the processor chooses to acquire declarations and definitions dynamically, that there be no side effects of such dynamic acquisition that would cause the results of [·assessment·](#key-va) to differ from that which would have been obtained from the same schema components acquired in bulk.
**Note:**the [·assessment·](#key-va) core is defined in terms of schema components at the abstract level, and no mention is made of the schema definition syntax (i.e. [<schema>](#element-schema)). Although many processors will acquire schemas in this format, others may operate on compiled representations, on a programmatic representation as exposed in some programming language, etc.
The obligation of a schema-aware processor as far as the [·assessment·](#key-va) core is concerned is to implement one or more of the options for [·assessment·](#key-va) given below in [Assessing Schema-Validity (§5.2)](#validation_outcome). Neither the choice of element information item for that [·assessment·](#key-va), nor which of the means of initiating [·assessment·](#key-va) are used, is within the scope of this specification.

Although [·assessment·](#key-va) is defined recursively, it is also intended to be implementable in streaming processors. Such processors may choose to incrementally assemble the schema during processing in response, for example, to encountering new namespaces. The implication of the invariants expressed above is that such incremental assembly must result in an [·assessment·](#key-va) outcome that is the *same*as would be given if [·assessment·](#key-va) were undertaken again with the final, fully assembled schema.

### <a id="layer2"></a>4.2 Layer 2: Schema Documents, Namespaces and Composition

4.2.1 [Basic concepts of schema construction and composition](#composition-concepts)
4.2.2 [Conditional inclusion](#cip)
4.2.3 [Assembling a schema for a single target namespace from multiple schema definition documents (<include>)](#compound-schema)
4.2.4 [Including modified component definitions (<redefine>)](#modify-schema)
4.2.5 [Overriding component definitions (<override>)](#override-schema)
4.2.6 [References to schema components across namespaces (<import>)](#composition-schemaImport)
4.2.6.1 [Licensing References to Components Across Namespaces](#composition-importLicenseReferences)
4.2.6.2 [Providing Hints for Imported Schema Document Locations](#schemaLocationHints)
The sub-sections of [Schema Component Details (§3)](#components) define an XML representation for type definitions and element declarations and so on, specifying their target namespace and collecting them into schema documents. The following sections describe how to assemble a complete schema from multiple sources.

**Note:**The core [·assessment·](#key-va) architecture requires that a complete schema with all the necessary declarations and definitions be available. This will sometimes involve resolving both instance → schema (or schema document) and schema-document → schema (or schema document) references. As observed earlier in [Conformance (§2.4)](#concepts-conformance), the precise mechanisms for resolving such references are expected to evolve over time. In support of such evolution, this specification observes the design principle that references from one schema document to a schema use mechanisms that directly parallel those used to reference a schema from an instance document.**Note:**In the sections below, "schemaLocation" really belongs at layer 3. For convenience, it is documented with the layer 2 mechanisms of import and include, with which it is closely associated.
#### <a id="composition-concepts"></a>4.2.1 Basic concepts of schema construction and composition

When a schema is assembled from multiple sources, those sources can include both [·schema documents·](#key-schemaDoc) and other sources of components. Several of the mechanisms described below allow a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc) to refer to a schema, either by giving the target namespace of its components, or indirectly, by referring to a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc) (and thus implicitly to the schema corresponding to that [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)).

The sections that follow appeal often to the following concepts.

<a id="key-pre-processing"></a>pre-processingThe pre-processing of a schema document before attempting to map its contents into components, or the pre-processing of a set of components before they take their final form among the components of a schema being constructed. The following sections define several forms of pre-processing, including: conditional-inclusion pre-processing, chameleon pre-processing, redefinition pre-processing, and override pre-processing. Conditional-inclusion pre-processing is always performed first; the sequence in which other forms of pre-processing are applied varies; details are given below. <a id="key-vc-preprocessing"></a>conditional-inclusion pre-processingThe process of applying to a schema document the filtering described in section [Conditional inclusion (§4.2.2)](#cip). This pre-processing is usually assumed silently; when it is explicitly described, then for a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)*D*, the result of conditional-inclusion pre-processing of *D*is written "*ci*(*D*)". <a id="key-imm-components"></a>immediate componentsThe immediate components of a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)*D*are the components corresponding to its definition and declaration [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element); also written "*immed*(*D*)".<a id="key-inter-sc-ref"></a>inter-schema-document referencesThe [<include>](#element-include), [<redefine>](#element-redefine), [<override>](#element-override), and [<import>](#element-import) elements defined below. <a id="key-corresponding-schema"></a>corresponding schemaThe schema corresponding to a given [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)*D*is the schema containing the built-in components, the [·automatically known·](#key-automatic) components automatically included in the schema by the schema processor, [·immed(D)·](#key-imm-components), and the components included by virtue of the inter-schema-document references included in *D*(for which see the rules below), and no other components. Also written "*schema*(*D*)".<a id="key-tns-f"></a>target namespaceThe target namespace specified in a schema document *D*; also written "*tns*(*D*)". <a id="key-chameleon-pre-processing"></a>chameleon pre-processingThe application, to a schema document *D*, of the transformation specified in [Transformation for Chameleon Inclusion (§F.1)](#chameleon-xslt), with respect to some namespace *N*, so as to convert it to use *N*as its target namespace; also written "*chameleon*(*N*,*D*)". <a id="key-override-pre-processing"></a>override pre-processingThe application, to a schema document *D*, of the overrides specified in an [<override>](#element-override) element *E*, using the rules specified in detail in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt); also written "*override*(*E*,*D*)". <a id="key-redefine-pre-processing"></a>redefine pre-processingThe application, to a schema *S*, of the redefinitions specified in an [<override>](#element-override) element *E*, using the rules specified in detail in [Including modified component definitions (<redefine>) (§4.2.4)](#modify-schema); also written "*redefine*(*E*,*S*)". **Note:**This specification defines several attributes to allow schema documents and XML instance documents to provide information about the location of relevant schema components. Experience has shown that the similarities and differences among these attributes can be elusive for some readers; a compact overview may be helpful. The `xsi:schemaLocation`and `xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation`attributes may validly appear on elements in an XML instance document. They are technically **hints**: conforming schema-aware processors may but need not attempt to dereference the schema documents named in these attributes and include the components described there in the schema used for validation. The `schemaLocation`attribute on the [<import>](#element-import) element in a schema document is similarly a hint: a conforming schema-aware processor may but need not attempt to dereference the schema document named by the attribute. The `schemaLocation`attributes on the [<include>](#element-include), [<override>](#element-override), and [<redefine>](#element-redefine) elements in a schema document, on the other hand, are not hints: conforming processors must attempt to de-reference the schema document named by the attribute. For non-empty [<redefine>](#element-redefine) elements, it is an error for the attempt to fail; otherwise, the attempt must be made but it is not an error for it to fail. In all cases, the use of the attribute in a document indicates the existence of a suitable schema document at the location indicated. This is stated explicitly below for some attributes, but not for all; where not stated explicitly, the inference follows implicitly from other rules.
#### <a id="cip"></a>4.2.2 Conditional inclusion

Whenever a conforming XSD processor reads a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc) in order to include the components defined in it in a schema, it first performs on the schema document the pre-processing described in this section.

Every element in the [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc) is examined to see whether any of the attributes `vc:minVersion`, `vc:maxVersion`, `vc:typeAvailable`, `vc:typeUnavailable`, `vc:facetAvailable`, or `vc:facetUnavailable`appear among its [[attributes]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element).

Where they appear, the attributes `vc:minVersion`and `vc:maxVersion`are treated as if declared with type `xs:decimal`, and their [·actual values·](#key-vv) are compared to a decimal value representing the version of XSD supported by the processor (here represented as a variable *V*). For processors conforming to this version of this specification, the value of *V*is 1.1.

If *V*is less than the value of `vc:minVersion`, or if *V*is greater than or equal to the value of `vc:maxVersion`, then the element on which the attribute appears is to be ignored, along with all its attributes and descendants. The effect is that portions of the schema document marked with `vc:minVersion`and/or `vc:maxVersion`are retained if `vc:minVersion`≤ *V*< `vc:maxVersion`.

Where they appear, the attributes `vc:typeAvailable`and `vc:typeUnavailable`are treated as if declared with type `list of xs:QName`, and the items in their [·actual values·](#key-vv) are checked to see whether they name types [·automatically known·](#key-automatic) to the processor. The attributes `vc:facetAvailable`and `vc:facetUnavailable`are similarly typed, and checked to see if they name facets supported by the processor.

If an element in a schema document has any of the following:
1. `vc:typeAvailable`= *T*, where any item in the [·actual value·](#key-vv)*T*is *not*the [expanded name](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of some type definition [·automatically known·](#key-automatic) to the processor
2. `vc:typeUnavailable`= *T*, where every item in the [·actual value·](#key-vv)*T*is the [expanded name](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of some type definition [·automatically known·](#key-automatic) to and supported by the processor
3. `vc:facetAvailable`= *F*, where any item in the [·actual value·](#key-vv)*F*is not the [expanded name](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of some facet known to and supported by the processor
4. `vc:facetUnavailable`= *F*, where every item in the [·actual value·](#key-vv)*F*is the [expanded name](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of some facet known to and supported by the processor
then the element on which the attribute appears is to be ignored, along with all its attributes and descendants.**Note:**It is expected that `vc:typeAvailable`etc. will be most useful in testing for [·implementation-defined·](#key-impl-defined) primitive datatypes and facets, or for derived types for which the processor supplies a definition automatically. The rules just given do not, however, attempt to restrict their use to such tests. If the `vc:typeAvailable`attribute is used with the [expanded name](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) associated with one of the built-in primitive datatypes, the datatype will (in a conforming processor) always be available, so the test is unlikely to filter out any elements, ever, from the schema document. But such a usage is not in itself an error. **Note:**The [expanded names](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of the built-in datatypes are as specified in [[XML Schema: Datatypes]](#ref-xsp2); the [expanded name](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of any [·implementation-defined·](#key-impl-defined) datatype is required by [[XML Schema: Datatypes]](#ref-xsp2) to be specified by the implementation. **Note:**The [expanded names](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of the facets (built-in or [·implementation-defined·](#key-impl-defined)) are the [expanded names](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-names11-20040204/#dt-expname) of the elements used in XSD schema documents to apply the facets, e.g. `xs:pattern`for the ***pattern***facet. **Note:**It is a consequence of the rules given above that if the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of `vc:typeAvailable`is the empty list (i.e. `vc:typeAvailable=""`), then the corresponding element is *not*ignored. (It does not list any type that is not available.) Conversely, if the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of `vc:typeUnavailable`is the empty list, then the corresponding element is ignored. Similar results hold for `vc:facetAvailable`and `vc:facetUnavailable`.
The pre-processing of a schema document *S1*results in a second schema document *S2*, identical to *S1*except that all elements and attributes in *S1*which are to be ignored are absent from *S2*. We write *S2*= *ci*(*S1*). If the `<schema>`element information item in *S1*is to be ignored, then *S2*is identical to *S1*except that any attributes other than `targetNamespace`, `vc:minVersion`or `vc:maxVersion`are removed from its [[attributes]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), and its [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) is the empty sequence. It is *S2*, not *S1*, which is required to conform to this specification.

**Note:**If *S1*contains no elements or attributes to be ignored, then *S1*and *S2*are identical.
Except where conditional-inclusion pre-processing is explicitly mentioned, references to [·schema documents·](#key-schemaDoc) elsewhere in this specification invariably refer to the result of the pre-processing step described here, not to its input, which need not, and in the general case will not, always conform to the rules for schema documents laid out in this specification.

ExampleSuppose some future version of XSD (say, version 3.2) introduces a new form of element declaration. A schema author might wish to exploit that new form of declaration if possible, but wish also to ensure that the schema document can be handled successfully by a processor written for XSD 1.1. In such a case, a schema document of the following form would be handled correctly by a conforming XSD 1.1 processor:
```
<xs:schema
  xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
  xmlns:vc="http://www.w3.org/2007/XMLSchema-versioning">

  <xs:element name="e" vc:minVersion="3.2">
    <!--* declaration suitable for 3.2
        * and later processors *-->
  </xs:element>
  <xs:element name="e"
    vc:minVersion="1.1"
    vc:maxVersion="3.2">
    <!--* declaration suitable for processors
        * supporting versions 1.1 through versions
        * up to (but not including) 3.2
        *-->
  </xs:element>
  ...
</xs:schema>
```

Even though the schema document as shown has two element declarations for element `e`and thus violates clause [2](#c-nmd) of constraint [Schema Properties Correct (§3.17.6.1)](#sch-props-correct), the pre-processing step described in this section filters out the first element declaration, making it possible for the resulting schema document to be valid and to conform to this specification. Note that the semantics of the `vc:maxVersion`attribute is "exclusive". This makes it easier for schema authors to use this feature without leaving gaps in the numeric ranges used to select version numbers. Example
Suppose that a processor supports an [·implementation-defined·](#key-impl-defined) primitive named `xpath_expression`in namespace "`http://example.org/extension_types`", and is presented with the following schema document:

```
<xs:schema
 xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
 xmlns:vc="http://www.w3.org/2007/XMLSchema-versioning"
 xmlns:tns="http://example.org/extension_types"
 targetNamespace="http://example.org/extension_types" >

 <xs:element vc:typeAvailable="tns:xpath_expression"
          name="e" type="tns:xpath_expression" />
 <xs:element vc:typeUnavailable="tns:xpath_expression"
          name="e" type="string" />
</xs:schema>
```

The effect of conditional inclusion is to include the first declaration for `e`and omit the second, so that the effective schema document, after pre-processing for conditional inclusion, is:

```
<xs:schema
 xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
 xmlns:vc="http://www.w3.org/2007/XMLSchema-versioning"
 xmlns:tns="http://example.org/extension_types"
 targetNamespace="http://example.org/extension_types" >

 <xs:element vc:typeAvailable="tns:xpath_expression"
          name="e" type="tns:xpath_expression" />
</xs:schema>
```

A processor which does not support type "tns:xpath_expression", by contrast, will use the other declaration for `e`: type in the namespace in question

```
<xs:schema
 xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
 xmlns:vc="http://www.w3.org/2007/XMLSchema-versioning"
 xmlns:tns="http://example.org/extension_types"
 targetNamespace="http://example.org/extension_types" >

 <xs:element vc:typeUnavailable="tns:xpath_expression"
          name="e" type="string" />
</xs:schema>
```

<a id="src-cip"></a>**Schema Representation Constraint: Conditional Inclusion Constraints**
Whenever the attribute `vc:minVersion`or `vc:maxVersion`appears on an element information item in a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc), its [·initial value·](#key-iv)must be locally [·valid·](#key-vn) with respect to `xs:decimal`as per [String Valid (§3.16.4)](#cvc-simple-type).Whenever any of the attributes `vc:typeAvailable`, `vc:typeUnavailable`, `vc:facetAvailable`, or `vc:facetUnavailable`, appears on an element information item in a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc), its [·initial value·](#key-iv)must be locally [·valid·](#key-vn) with respect to a simple type definition with [{variety}](#std-variety) = ***list***and [{item type definition}](#std-item_type_definition) = `xs:QName`, as per [String Valid (§3.16.4)](#cvc-simple-type). Any attribute from the `vc:`namespace that appears on an element information item in a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)should be one of the attributes described elsewhere in this document (i.e. one of `vc:minVersion`, `vc:maxVersion`, `vc:typeAvailable`, `vc:typeUnavailable`, `vc:facetAvailable`, or `vc:facetUnavailable`). **Note:**Processors are encouraged to issue warnings about `vc:`attributes other than those named, but it is not an error for such attributes to appear in a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc). The rule just given is formulated with a "should" and not a "must" in order to preserve the ability of future versions of this specification to add new attributes to the schema-versioning namespace.
#### <a id="compound-schema"></a>4.2.3 Assembling a schema for a single target namespace from multiple schema definition documents (<include>)

Schema components for a single target namespace can be assembled from several [·schema documents·](#key-schemaDoc), that is several [<schema>](#element-schema) element information items:

XML Representation Summary: `include`Element Information Item<a id="element-include"></a><include
id = [ID](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#ID)
**schemaLocation**= [anyURI](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#anyURI)
*{any attributes with non-schema namespace . . .}*>
*Content: *([annotation](#element-annotation)?)
</include>
A [<schema>](#element-schema) information item may contain any number of [<include>](#element-include) elements. Their `schemaLocation`attributes, consisting of a URI reference, identify other [·schema documents·](#key-schemaDoc), that is [<schema>](#element-schema) information items.

If two [<include>](#element-include) elements specify the same schema location (after resolving relative URI references) then they refer to the same schema document. If they specify different schema locations, then they refer to different schema documents, unless the implementation is able to determine that the two URIs are references to the same resource.

If a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)*D*1 contains one or more [<include>](#element-include) elements, then *schema*(*D*1) contains not only *immed*(*D*1) but also all the components of *schema*(*D*2), for each [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc)*D*2 identified by an [<include>](#element-include) element child of *D*1. Such included schema documents *D*2must either (a) have the same `targetNamespace`as *D*1, or (b) no `targetNamespace`at all, in which case the components included in *schema*(*D*1) are not those of *schema*(*D*2) itself, but instead those of *schema*(*chameleon*(*tns*(*D*1),*D*2)), that is, the schema corresponding to the result of applying chameleon pre-processing to *D*2 to convert it to target namespace *tns*(*D*1).

<a id="src-include"></a>**Schema Representation Constraint: Inclusion Constraints and Semantics**
In addition to the conditions imposed on [<include>](#element-include) element information items by the schema for schema documents, **all**of the following also apply:1<a id="c-ins"></a>If the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `schemaLocation`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) successfully resolves **one or more**of the following is true:1.1<a id="c-vxd"></a>It resolves to (a fragment of) a resource which is an XML document (of type `application/xml`or `text/xml`with an XML declaration for preference, but this is not required), which in turn corresponds to a [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item in a well-formed information set.1.2 It resolves to a [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item in a well-formed information set. In either case call the [<include>](#element-include)d [<schema>](#element-schema) item **D2**and the [<include>](#element-include)ing item's parent [<schema>](#element-schema) item **D1**.2 **One**of the following must be true:2.1<a id="c-normi"></a>**D2**has a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), and its [·actual value·](#key-vv) is identical to the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of **D1**(which must have such an [[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element)).2.2<a id="c-normi2"></a>Neither **D2**nor **D1**have a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element).2.3<a id="c-chami"></a>**D2**has no `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) (but **D1**does).2.4 **D2**does not exist (e.g. because the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `schemaLocation`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) does not resolve successfully).3 The appropriate **case**among the following must be true:3.1 **If **clause [2.1](#c-normi) or clause [2.2](#c-normi2) above is satisfied, **then ****all**of the following are true:3.1.1 **D2**corresponds to a conforming schema (call it **S2**). 3.1.2<a id="c-incl-incl"></a> The schema corresponding to **D1**includes not only definitions or declarations corresponding to the appropriate members of its own [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), but also components identical to all the [·schema components·](#c) of **S2**(with the possible exception of its [Schema](#s) component). 3.2<a id="c-docham"></a>**If **clause [2.3](#c-chami) above is satisfied, **then ****all**of the following are true:3.2.1<a id="c-chamvalidi"></a> Let **D2′**be a [<schema>](#element-schema) information item obtained by performing on **D2**the transformation specified in [Transformation for Chameleon Inclusion (§F.1)](#chameleon-xslt); **D2′**corresponds to a conforming schema (call it **S2**). **Note:**The transformation in [Transformation for Chameleon Inclusion (§F.1)](#chameleon-xslt) (a) adds a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) to **D2**, whose value is the same as that of the `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of **D1**, and (b) updates all unqualified QName references so that their namespace names become the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element). Implementations need not use the [[XSLT 2.0]](#bib-xslt2) stylesheet given in [Transformation for Chameleon Inclusion (§F.1)](#chameleon-xslt), as long as an equivalent result is produced. In particular, different algorithms for generating a unique namespace prefix may be used, even if they produce different results. 3.2.2<a id="c-incl-ns-capture"></a> The schema corresponding to **D1**includes not only definitions or declarations corresponding to the appropriate members of its own [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), but also components identical to all the [·schema components·](#c) of **S2**(with the possible exception of its [Schema](#s) component). **Note:**The above rule applies recursively. For example, if **A**includes **B**and **B**includes **C**, where **A**has a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), but neither **B**nor **C**does, then the effect is as if **A**included **B'**and **B'**included **C'**, where **B'**and **C'**are identical to **B**and **C**respectively, except that they both have a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) the same as **A**'s. **Note:**In this case, it is **D2′**, not **D2**, which is required by clause [3.2.1](#c-chamvalidi) to correspond to a conforming schema. In particular, it is not an error for **D2**to fail to satisfy all of the constraints governing schema documents, while it *is*an error if **D2′**fails to satisfy them. **Note:**If **D2**imports the target namespace of **D1**, then the effect of clause [3.2](#c-docham) will be to cause an error owing to the violation of clause [1](#src-import-noselfimport) of [Import Constraints and Semantics (§4.2.6.2)](#src-import) (which forbids a schema document to import its own target namespace). Other constraint violations may also be brought about; caution is advised. It is *not*an error for the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `schemaLocation`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) to fail to resolve at all, in which case the corresponding inclusion must not be performed. It *is*an error for it to resolve but the rest of clause 1 above to fail to be satisfied. Failure to resolve is likely to cause less than complete [·assessment·](#key-va) outcomes, of course.As discussed in [Missing Sub-components (§5.3)](#conformance-missing), [·QName·](#gloss-QName)s in XML representations will sometimes fail to [·resolve·](#src-resolve), rendering components incomplete and unusable because of missing subcomponents. During schema construction, implementations must retain [·QName·](#gloss-QName) values for such references, in case an appropriately-named component becomes available to discharge the reference by the time it is actually needed. [·Absent·](#key-null) target [·namespace name·](#q-uri)s of such as-yet unresolved reference [·QName·](#gloss-QName)s in [<include>](#element-include)d components must also be converted if clause [3.2](#c-docham) is satisfied.**Note:**The above is carefully worded so that multiple [<include>](#element-include)ing of the same schema document will not constitute a violation of clause [2](#c-nmd) of [Schema Properties Correct (§3.17.6.1)](#sch-props-correct), but applications are allowed, indeed encouraged, to avoid [<include>](#element-include)ing the same schema document more than once to forestall the necessity of establishing identity component by component.
If there is a sequence of schema documents *S1*, *S2*, ... *Sn*, and a sequence of [<include>](#element-include) elements *E1*, *E2*, ... *En*, such that each *S**i*contains the corresponding *E**i*, and each *E**i*(where *i*< *n*) points to schema document *S**i*+ 1, and *En*points to *S1*(i.e. if there is a cycle in the relation defined by the [<include>](#element-include) element), then the same schema corresponds to all of the schema documents *S1*, ... *Sn*in the cycle, and it includes the same components as the schema corresponding to *S1*in the similar case where *Sn*has no [<include>](#element-include) element pointing at *S1*.

**Note:**Informally: cycles of [<include>](#element-include) elements are legal, and processors should guard against infinite loops.
#### <a id="modify-schema"></a>4.2.4 Including modified component definitions (<redefine>)

**Note:**The redefinition feature described in the remainder of this section is [·deprecated·](#key-deprecated) and may be removed from future versions of this specification. Schema authors are encouraged to avoid its use in cases where interoperability or compatibility with later versions of this specification are important.
In order to provide some support for evolution and versioning, it is possible to incorporate components corresponding to a schema document *with modifications*. The modifications have a pervasive impact, that is, only the redefined components are used, even when referenced from other incorporated components, whether redefined themselves or not.

XML Representation Summary: `redefine`Element Information Item<a id="element-redefine"></a><redefine
id = [ID](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#ID)
**schemaLocation**= [anyURI](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#anyURI)
*{any attributes with non-schema namespace . . .}*>
*Content: *([annotation](#element-annotation) | ([simpleType](#element-simpleType) | [complexType](#element-complexType) | [group](#element-group) | [attributeGroup](#element-attributeGroup)))*
</redefine>
A [<schema>](#element-schema) information item may contain any number of [<redefine>](#element-redefine) elements. Their `schemaLocation`attributes, consisting of a URI reference, identify other [·schema documents·](#key-schemaDoc), that is [<schema>](#element-schema) information items.

If a schema document *D*1 contains a [<redefine>](#element-redefine) element *E*pointing to some schema document *D*2, then *schema*(*D*1) contains not only the components in *immed*(*D*1), but also all the components (with the exception, in most cases, of the schema-as-a-whole component) of *redefine*(*E*,*schema*(*D*2)). For any document *D*2 pointed at by a [<redefine>](#element-redefine) element in *D*1, it must be the case either (a) that *tns*(*D*1) = *tns*(*D*2) or else (b) that *tns*(*D*2) is [·absent·](#key-null), in which case *schema*(*D*1) includes not *redefine*(*E*,*schema*(*D*2)) itself but *redefine*(*E*,*schema*(*chameleon*(*tns*(*D*1),*D*2))). That is, the redefinition pre-processing is applied not to the schema corresponding to *D*2 but instead to the schema corresponding to the schema document *chameleon*(*tns*(*D*1),*D*2), which is the result of applying chameleon pre-processing to *D*2 to convert it to target namespace *tns*(*D*1).

The definitions within the [<redefine>](#element-redefine) element itself are restricted to be redefinitions of components from the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d schema document, *in terms of themselves*. That is,
- Type definitions must use themselves as their base type definition;
- Attribute group definitions and model group definitions must be supersets or subsets of their original definitions, either by including exactly one reference to themselves or by containing only (possibly restricted) components which appear in a corresponding way in their [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d selves.
Not all the components of the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d schema document need be redefined.
This mechanism is intended to provide a declarative and modular approach to schema modification, with functionality no different except in scope from what would be achieved by wholesale text copying and redefinition by editing. In particular redefining a type is not guaranteed to be side-effect free: it can have unexpected impacts on other type definitions which are based on the redefined one, even to the extent that some such definitions become ill-formed.

**Note:**The pervasive impact of redefinition reinforces the need for implementations to adopt some form of lazy or 'just-in-time' approach to component construction, which is also called for in order to avoid inappropriate dependencies on the order in which definitions and references appear in (collections of) schema documents.Example
```
v1.xsd:
 <xs:complexType name="personName">
  <xs:sequence>
   <xs:element name="title" minOccurs="0"/>
   <xs:element name="forename" minOccurs="0" maxOccurs="unbounded"/>
  </xs:sequence>
 </xs:complexType>

 <xs:element name="addressee" type="personName"/>

v2.xsd:
 <xs:redefine schemaLocation="v1.xsd">
  <xs:complexType name="personName">
   <xs:complexContent>
    <xs:extension base="personName">
     <xs:sequence>
      <xs:element name="generation" minOccurs="0"/>
     </xs:sequence>
    </xs:extension>
   </xs:complexContent>
  </xs:complexType>
 </xs:redefine>

 <xs:element name="author" type="personName"/>

```

The schema corresponding to `v2.xsd`has everything specified by `v1.xsd`, with the `personName`type redefined, as well as everything it specifies itself. According to this schema, elements constrained by the `personName`type may end with a `generation`element. This includes not only the `author`element, but also the `addressee`element.<a id="src-redefine"></a>**Schema Representation Constraint: Redefinition Constraints and Semantics**
In addition to the conditions imposed on [<redefine>](#element-redefine) element information items by the schema for schema documents **all**of the following also apply:1 If there are any element information items among the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) other than [<annotation>](#element-annotation) then the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `schemaLocation`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element)must successfully resolve.2<a id="redef-resolves"></a>If the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `schemaLocation`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) successfully resolves **one or more**of the following is true:2.1 it resolves to (a fragment of) a resource which is an XML document (see clause [1.1](#c-vxd) of [Inclusion Constraints and Semantics (§4.2.3)](#src-include)), which in turn corresponds to a [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item in a well-formed information set.2.2 It resolves to a [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item in a well-formed information set. In either case call the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d [<schema>](#element-schema) item **D2**and the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)ing item's parent [<schema>](#element-schema) item **D1**.3 **One**of the following must be true:3.1<a id="c-normir"></a>**D2**has a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), and its [·actual value·](#key-vv) is identical to the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of **D1**(which must have such an [[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element)).3.2<a id="c-normi2r"></a>Neither **D2**nor **D1**have a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element).3.3<a id="c-chamir"></a>**D2**has no `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) (but **D1**does).4 The appropriate **case**among the following must be true:4.1 **If **clause [3.1](#c-normir) or clause [3.2](#c-normi2r) above is satisfied, **then **4.1.1 **D2**corresponds to a conforming schema (call it **S2**). 4.1.2 The schema corresponding to **D1**includes not only definitions or declarations corresponding to the appropriate members of its own [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), but also components identical to all the [·schema components·](#c) of **S2**, with the exception of those explicitly redefined (see [Individual Component Redefinition (§4.2.4)](#src-expredef) below and with the possible exception of the [Schema](#s) component of **S2**)). 4.2<a id="c-dochamr"></a>**If **clause [3.3](#c-chamir) above is satisfied, **then **4.2.1<a id="c-chamvalidr"></a> Let **D2′**be a [<schema>](#element-schema) information item obtained by performing on **D2**the transformation specified in [Transformation for Chameleon Inclusion (§F.1)](#chameleon-xslt); **D2′**corresponds to a conforming schema (call it **S2**). 4.2.2 The schema corresponding to **D1**includes not only definitions or declarations corresponding to the appropriate members of its own [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), but also components identical to all the [·schema components·](#c) of **S2**, with the exception of those explicitly redefined (see [Individual Component Redefinition (§4.2.4)](#src-expredef) below). **Note:**In this case, it is **D2′**and not **D2**, which is required by clause [4.2.1](#c-chamvalidr) to correspond to a conforming schema. In particular, it is not an error for **D2**to fail to satisfy all of the constraints governing schema documents, while it *is*an error if **D2′**fails to satisfy them. 5 Within the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), each [<simpleType>](#element-simpleType)must have a [<restriction>](#element-restriction) among its [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) and each [<complexType>](#element-complexType)must have a `restriction`or `extension`among its grand-[[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of whose `base`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element)must be the same as the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of its own `name`attribute plus target namespace;6 Within the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), for each [<group>](#element-group) the appropriate **case**among the following must be true:6.1 **If **it has a [<group>](#element-group) among its contents at some level the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of whose `ref`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) is the same as the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of its own `name`attribute plus target namespace and that [<group>](#element-group) does not have an [<element>](#element-element) ancestor, **then ****all**of the following are true:6.1.1 It has exactly one such group.6.1.2 The [·actual value·](#key-vv) of both that group's `minOccurs`and `maxOccurs`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) is `1`(or [·absent·](#key-null)).6.2 **If **it has no such self-reference, **then ****all**of the following are true:6.2.1 The [·actual value·](#key-vv) of its own `name`attribute plus target namespace successfully [·resolves·](#src-resolve) to a model group definition in **S2**.6.2.2 <a id="anchor6685D"></a> The [{model group}](#mgd-model_group) of the model group definition which corresponds to it per [XML Representation of Model Group Definition Schema Components (§3.7.2)](#declare-namedModelGroup) accepts a subset of the element sequences accepted by that model group definition in **S2**.7 Within the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), for each [<attributeGroup>](#element-simpleContent..attributeGroup) the appropriate **case**among the following must be true:7.1 **If **it has an [<attributeGroup>](#element-simpleContent..attributeGroup) among its contents the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of whose `ref`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) is the same as the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of its own `name`attribute plus target namespace, **then **it has exactly one such group.7.2<a id="c-agrere"></a>**If **it has no such self-reference, **then ****all**of the following are true:7.2.1 The [·actual value·](#key-vv) of its own `name`attribute plus target namespace successfully [·resolves·](#src-resolve) to an attribute group definition in **S2**.7.2.2 The [{attribute uses}](#agd-attribute_uses) and [{attribute wildcard}](#agd-attribute_wildcard) of the attribute group definition which corresponds to it per [XML Representation of Attribute Group Definition Schema Components (§3.6.2)](#declare-attributeGroup) viewed as the [{attribute uses}](#ctd-attribute_uses) and [{attribute wildcard}](#ctd-attribute_wildcard) of a [Complex Type Definition](#ctd) and the [{attribute uses}](#agd-attribute_uses) and [{attribute wildcard}](#agd-attribute_wildcard) of that attribute group definition in **S2**viewed as the [{attribute uses}](#ctd-attribute_uses) and [{attribute wildcard}](#ctd-attribute_wildcard) of the [{base type definition}](#ctd-base_type_definition) satisfy clause [3](#c-ran) of [Derivation Valid (Restriction, Complex) (§3.4.6.3)](#derivation-ok-restriction).**Note:**An attribute group restrictively redefined per clause [7.2](#c-agrere) corresponds to an attribute group whose [{attribute uses}](#agd-attribute_uses) consist all and only of those attribute uses corresponding to [<attribute>](#element-attribute)s explicitly present among the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)ing [<attributeGroup>](#element-simpleContent..attributeGroup). No inheritance from the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d attribute group occurs. Its [{attribute wildcard}](#agd-attribute_wildcard) is similarly based purely on an explicit [<anyAttribute>](#element-anyAttribute), if present.<a id="src-expredef"></a>**Schema Representation Constraint: Individual Component Redefinition**
Corresponding to each non-[<annotation>](#element-annotation) member of the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of a [<redefine>](#element-redefine) there are one or two schema components in the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)ing schema:1 The [<simpleType>](#element-simpleType) and [<complexType>](#element-complexType)[[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) information items each correspond to two components:1.1<a id="icr-c11"></a>One component which corresponds to the top-level definition item with the same `name`in the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d schema document, as defined in [Schema Component Details (§3)](#components), except that its {name} is [·absent·](#key-null) and its {context} is the redefining component, as defined in clause [1.2](#icr-c12) below;1.2<a id="icr-c12"></a>One component which corresponds to the information item itself, as defined in [Schema Component Details (§3)](#components), except that its {base type definition} is the component defined in clause [1.1](#icr-c11) above.This pairing ensures the coherence constraints on type definitions are respected, while at the same time achieving the desired effect, namely that references to names of redefined components in both the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)ing and [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d schema documents [·resolve·](#src-resolve) to the redefined component as specified in 1.2 above.2 The [<group>](#element-group) and [<attributeGroup>](#element-simpleContent..attributeGroup)[[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) each correspond to a single component, as defined in [Schema Component Details (§3)](#components), except that if and when a self-reference based on a `ref`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) whose [·actual value·](#key-vv) is the same as the item's `name`plus target namespace is [·resolved·](#src-resolve), a component which corresponds to the top-level definition item of that name and the appropriate kind in **S2**is used.In all cases there must be a top-level definition item of the appropriate name and kind in the [<redefine>](#element-redefine)d schema document.**Note:**The above is carefully worded so that multiple equivalent [<redefine>](#element-redefine)ing of the same schema document will not constitute a violation of clause [2](#c-nmd) of [Schema Properties Correct (§3.17.6.1)](#sch-props-correct), but applications are allowed, indeed encouraged, to avoid [<redefine>](#element-redefine)ing the same schema document in the same way more than once to forestall the necessity of establishing identity component by component (although this will have to be done for the individual redefinitions themselves).
#### <a id="override-schema"></a>4.2.5 Overriding component definitions (<override>)

The `<redefine>`construct defined in [Including modified component definitions (<redefine>) (§4.2.4)](#modify-schema) is useful in schema evolution and versioning, when it is desirable to have some guaranteed restriction or extension relation between the old component and the redefined component. But there are occasions when the schema author simply wants to replace old components with new ones without any constraint. Also, existing XSD processors have implemented conflicting and non-interoperable interpretations of [<redefine>](#element-redefine), and the [<redefine>](#element-redefine) construct is [·deprecated·](#key-deprecated). The [<override>](#element-override) construct defined in this section allows such unconstrained replacement.

**Note:**The name of the [<override>](#element-override) element has nothing to do with the use of the term "[·override·](#key-overrides)" to denote the relation between an [·instance-specified type definition·](#key-itd) and another type. The two mechanisms are distinct and unrelated.XML Representation Summary: `override`Element Information Item<a id="element-override"></a><override
id = [ID](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#ID)
**schemaLocation**= [anyURI](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#anyURI)
*{any attributes with non-schema namespace . . .}*>
*Content: *([annotation](#element-annotation) | ([simpleType](#element-simpleType) | [complexType](#element-complexType) | [group](#element-group) | [attributeGroup](#element-attributeGroup) | [element](#element-element) | [attribute](#element-attribute) | [notation](#element-notation)))*
</override>
A [<schema>](#element-schema) information item may contain any number of [<override>](#element-override) elements. Their `schemaLocation`attributes, consisting of a URI reference, identify ("point to") other [·schema documents·](#key-schemaDoc), that is [<schema>](#element-schema) information items.

If a schema document *D**new*contains an [<override>](#element-override) element *E*pointing to some schema document *D**old*, then *schema*(*D**new*) contains not only the components in *immed*(*D**new*), but also the components in *schema*(*override*(*E*,*D**old*)). For all such schema documents *D**old*, it must be the case either (a) that *tns*(*D**old*) = *tns*(*D**new*), or (b) that *tns*(*D**old*) is [·absent·](#key-null), in which case *schema*(*D**new*) contains not the components in *schema*(*override*(*E*,*D**old*)), but those in *schema*(*override*(*E*,*chameleon*(*tns*(*D**new*),*D**old*))). That is, before the override pre-processsing is applied, chameleon pre-processing is applied to *D**old*to convert it to target namespace *tns*(*D**new*); the override pre-processing is applied to the result, namely *chameleon*(*tns*(*D**new*),*D**old*).

**Note:**If the above definition is naively translated into an algorithm, the algorithm may fail to terminate in the case where the graph of `include`and `override`references among schema documents contains cycles. To guarantee termination, the algorithm must detect when it has reached closure, that is, when further computation will have no effect on the outcome. In particular, it is useful to recognize (a) that it is possible to terminate as soon as conflicting components have been generated (for example, two different type definitions with the same name), and (b) that when *override*(*E*, *D*) (for some *E*and *D*) is equivalent to *D*, no new schema components will be contributed by further processing: this can be detected either by comparing the input and output of the override transformation using a comparator such as the [[XPath 2.0]](#bib-xpath2)`fn:deep-equal`function, or by observing the conditions that cause *override*(*E*, *D*) to be idempotent, for example the fact that *E*is empty.
The children of the [<override>](#element-override) element may override any source declarations for [·named·](#key-compName) components which appear among the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of the [<schema>](#element-schema), [<redefine>](#element-redefine), or [<override>](#element-override) elements in the [·target set·](#key-targetset) of the [<override>](#element-override) element information item..

<a id="key-targetset"></a>[Definition:]The **target set**of an [<override>](#element-override) element information item *E*contains **all**of the following:1 The schema document identified by the `schemaLocation`attribute of *E*.2 The schema document identified by the `schemaLocation`attribute of any [<override>](#element-override) element information item in a schema document contained in the [·target set·](#key-targetset) of *E*.3 The schema document identified by the `schemaLocation`attribute of any [<include>](#element-include) element information item in a schema document contained in the [·target set·](#key-targetset) of *E*. The **target set**of *E*contains no other schema documents. **Note:**The [·target set·](#key-targetset) of an [<override>](#element-override) element is the transitive closure of the union of the inclusion relation (which contains the pair (*S1*, *S2*) if and only if *S1*contains an [<include>](#element-include) element pointing to *S2*) and the override relation (which contains the pair (*S1*, *S2*) if and only if *S1*contains an [<override>](#element-override) element pointing to *S2*). It does not include schema documents which are pointed to by [<import>](#element-import) or [<redefine>](#element-redefine) elements, unless they are also pointed to by [<include>](#element-include) or [<override>](#element-override) elements in the relevant schema documents. Source declarations not present in the target set of *E*cannot be overridden, even if they are present in other schema documents consulted in the creation of the schema (e.g. in schema documents pointed to by a [<redefine>](#element-redefine) element). It is not an error for an [<override>](#element-override) element to contain a source declaration which matches nothing in the target set, but it will be ignored; in particular, it will not succeed in overriding source declarations in schema documents not part of the target set. **Note:**The transformation described in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt) translates both [<override>](#element-override) and [<include>](#element-include) elements into [<override>](#element-override) elements. These [<override>](#element-override) elements will, in turn, be handled by transforming their target schema documents. Since every schema document in the target set of the original [<override>](#element-override) element is the target of either an [<override>](#element-override) element or of an [<include>](#element-include) element, it follows that the transformation described in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt) will be applied to every schema document in the target set. **Note:**It is *not*forbidden for the schema document *D*containing an [<override>](#element-override) element *E*to be in the [·target set·](#key-targetset) of *E*.If applying the override transformation specified in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt) to *D*and *E*results in a schema document equivalent to *D*(e.g. when none of the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of *D*, or of any [<redefine>](#element-redefine) and [<override>](#element-override) elements in *D*match any of the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of *E*, except for the [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of *E*themselves), then the effect is the same as for a cyclic set of [<include>](#element-include) references, or as for multiple inclusions of the same document (as described in the note at the end of [Assembling a schema for a single target namespace from multiple schema definition documents (<include>) (§4.2.3)](#compound-schema)). If applying the override transformation to *D*and *E*changes any of the XML representations of components, then the effect of *D*being in the [·target set·](#key-targetset) of *E*is the same as if two different schema documents containing conflicting definitions for the same components were included. ("As if" is inexact; in this case what happens is, precisely, that two schema documents with conflicting contents are included.)
The definitions within the [<override>](#element-override) element itself are *not*required to be similar in any way to the source declarations being overridden. Not all the source declarations of the overridden schema document need be overridden.

As this mechanism is very similar to [<redefine>](#element-redefine), many similar kinds of caution need to be taken in using [<override>](#element-override). Please refer to [Including modified component definitions (<redefine>) (§4.2.4)](#modify-schema) for details.

Example
```
v1.xsd:
 <xs:complexType name="personName">
  <xs:sequence>
   <xs:element name="firstName"/>
   <xs:element name="lastName"/>
  </xs:sequence>
 </xs:complexType>

 <xs:element name="addressee" type="personName"/>

v2.xsd:
 <xs:override schemaLocation="v1.xsd">
  <xs:complexType name="personName">
   <xs:sequence>
    <xs:element name="givenName"/>
    <xs:element name="surname"/>
   </xs:sequence>
  </xs:complexType>
 </xs:override>

 <xs:element name="author" type="personName"/>

```

The schema corresponding to `v1.xsd`has a complex type named `personName`with a sequence of `firstName`and `lastName`children. The schema corresponding to `v2.xsd`overrides `personName`, by providing a different sequence of element children. All elements with the `personName`type are now constrained to have the sequence of `givenName`and `surname`. This includes not only the `author`element, but also the `addressee`element.<a id="src-override"></a>**Schema Representation Constraint: Override Constraints and Semantics**
In addition to the conditions imposed on [<override>](#element-override) element information items by the schema for schema documents **all**of the following also apply:1 If the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `schemaLocation`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) successfully resolves **one or more**of the following is true:1.1 It resolves to (a fragment of) a resource which is an XML document (see clause [1.1](#c-vxd) of [Inclusion Constraints and Semantics (§4.2.3)](#src-include)), which in turn corresponds to a [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item in a well-formed information set. 1.2 It resolves to a [<schema>](#element-schema) element information item in a well-formed information set. In either case call the overridden [<schema>](#element-schema) item *D**old*and the overriding item's parent [<schema>](#element-schema) item *D**new*. 2 **One**of the following must be true:2.1<a id="c-o-normir"></a>*D**old*has a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), and its [·actual value·](#key-vv) is identical to the [·actual value·](#key-vv) of the `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) of *D**new*(which must have such an [[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element)). 2.2<a id="c-o-normi2r"></a> Neither *D**old*nor *D**new*have a `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element). 2.3<a id="c-o-chamir"></a>*D**old*has no `targetNamespace`[[attribute]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element) (but *D**new*does). 3 The appropriate **case**among the following must be true:3.1 **If **clause [2.1](#c-o-normir) or clause [2.2](#c-o-normi2r) above is satisfied, **then **3.1.1<a id="c-e1e2"></a> Let *Dold′*be a [<schema>](#element-schema) information item obtained by performing on *D**old*the transformation specified in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt). Then *Dold′*corresponds to a conforming schema (call it *S**old*). 3.1.2 The [<override>](#element-override) element in schema document *D**new*pointing to *D**old*is replaced by an [<include>](#element-include) element pointing to *Dold′*and the inclusion is handled as described in [Assembling a schema for a single target namespace from multiple schema definition documents (<include>) (§4.2.3)](#compound-schema). **Note:**It is not necessary to perform a literal replacement of the [<override>](#element-override) element in *D**new*with an [<include>](#element-include) element; any implementation technique can be used as long as it produces the required result. **Note:**One effect of the rule just given is that the schema corresponding to *D**new*includes not only definitions or declarations corresponding to the appropriate members of its own [[children]](https://www.w3.org/TR/xml-infoset/#infoitem.element), but also components identical to all the [·schema components·](#c) of *S**old*(with the possible exception of the [Schema](#s) component of *S**old*). **Note:**Another effect is that if schema document *A*contains a source declaration for a component *E*, and schema document *B*overrides *A*with its own declaration for *E*, and schema document *C*in turn overrides *B*with a third declaration for *E*, then in calculating *schema*(*C*) 3.1.2.1 First, the override of *B*by *C*is handled. The resulting schema document (*B*′, a modified version of *B*) still contains an [<override>](#element-override) element referring to *A*, but the declaration for *E*contained in it has been replaced by that specified in *C*.3.1.2.2 Then, the override of *A*by *B*′ (the modified version of *B*) is handled.3.1.2.3 The resulting version of *A*, containing the declaration for *E*originally present in *C*, is included by *B*′, which is itself included by *C*. 3.1.2.4 The resulting schema contains the version of *E*originally specified in schema document *C*. (The references to "first" and "then" here refer to the logical precedence of operations, not to a required order in which implementations are required to perform particular tasks.) 3.2 **If **clause [2.3](#c-o-chamir) above is satisfied, **then **3.2.1<a id="c-o-chamvalidr"></a> Let *Dold′*be a [<schema>](#element-schema) information item obtained by performing on *D**old*first the transformation specified in [Transformation for Chameleon Inclusion (§F.1)](#chameleon-xslt) and then the transformation specified in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt). Then *Dold′*corresponds to a conforming schema (call it **S2**). 3.2.2 The [<override>](#element-override) element in schema document *D**new*pointing to *D**old*is replaced by an [<include>](#element-include) element pointing to *Dold′*and the inclusion is handled as described in [Assembling a schema for a single target namespace from multiple schema definition documents (<include>) (§4.2.3)](#compound-schema). **Note:**The effect of applying the stylesheet in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt) is to make *Dold′*identical to *D**old*except that some elements in *D**old*are replaced or modified, as described in [Transformation for xs:override (§F.2)](#override-xslt). Implementations do not have to use [[XSLT 2.0]](#bib-xslt2) transformation, as long as the same result is produced.**Note:**It is *Dold′*and not *D**old*, which is required to correspond to a conforming schema. In particular, it is not an error for *D**old*to fail to satisfy all of the constraints governing schema documents, while it *is*an error if *Dold′*fails to satisfy them. <a id="anchor10652"></a>**Note:**The effect of override pre-processing is that any declarations and definitions contained within an [<override>](#element-override) will be substituted for matching declarations and definitions within the target set; the resulting schema documents will then be processed normally, as described in the relevant portions of this specification. This has the effect that the rules for document-level defaults (`elementFormDefault`, `attributeFormDefault`, `blockDefault`, `finalDefault`, and so on) are applied not in the context of the document containing the [<override>](#element-override) (*D**new*) but in the context of the document containing the original overridden declaration or definition (*D**old*). Unexpected results may be minimized if the children of an [<override>](#element-override) are made independent of the document-level defaults by explicitly specifying the desired values for the properties in question. **Note:**In [<redefine>](#element-redefine), components are allowed or required to refer to themselves. There is no similar special treatment in [<override>](#element-override). Overriding components are constructed as if the overridden components had never existed. **Note:**The above is carefully worded so that multiple equivalent overrides of the same schema document will not constitute a violation of clause [2](#c-nmd) of [Schema Properties Correct (§3.17.6.1)](#sch-props-correct), but applications are allowed, indeed encouraged, to avoid overriding the same schema document in the same way more than once to forestall the necessity of establishing identity component by component. **Note:**It is a consequence of the semantics of inclusion, as defined in [Inclusion Constraints and Semantics (§4.2.3)](#src-include) (in particular clause [3.1.2](#c-incl-incl) and clause [3.2.2](#c-incl-ns-capture)); redefinition, as defined in [Including modified component definitions (<redefine>) (§4.2.4)](#modify-schema); import, as defined in [References to schema components across namespaces (<import>) (§4.2.6)](#composition-schemaImport); and overriding, as defined in this section, that if the same schema document is both (a) included, imported, or redefined, and (b) non-vacuously overridden, or if the same schema document overridden twice in different ways, then the resulting schema will have duplicate and conflicting versions of some components and will not be conforming, just as if two different schema documents had been included, with different declarations for the same [·named·](#key-compName) components.
#### <a id="composition-schemaImport"></a>4.2.6 References to schema components across namespaces (<import>)

As described in [XSD Abstract Data Model (§2.2)](#concepts-data-model), every top-level schema component is associated with a target namespace (or, explicitly, with none). Furthermore, each schema document carries on its [<schema>](#element-schema) element at most one `targetNamespace`attribute associating that document with a target namespace. This section sets out the syntax and mechanisms by which references may be made from within a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc) to components outside that document's target namespace. Also included within the same syntax is an optional facility for suggesting the URI of a [·schema document·](#key-schemaDoc) containing definitions and declarations for components from the foreign target namespace.

**Note:**Some users of version 1.0 of this specification [[XSD 1.0 2E]](#schema2e-1) have mistakenly assumed that the primary purpose of the [<import>](#element-import) is to cause retrieval of a resource identified by the `schemaLocation`attribute. Although the function of [<import>](#element-import) is unchanged in this version, the presentation below has been reorganized to clarify the two separate purposes served by [<import>](#element-import), namely (1) to license references, within a schema document, to components in the imported namespace, and (2) to provide information about the location of schema documents for imported namespaces. XML Representation Summary: `import`Element Information Item<a id="element-import"></a><import
id = [ID](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#ID)
namespace = [anyURI](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#anyURI)
schemaLocation = [anyURI](https://www.w3.org/TR/2012/REC-xmlschema11-2-20120405/datatypes.html#anyURI)
*{any attributes with non-schema namespace . . .}*>
*Content: *([annotation](#element-annotation)?)
</import>
The [<import>](#element-import) element information item identifies namespaces used in external references, i.e. those whose [·QName·](#gloss-QName) identifies them as coming from a different namespace (or none) than the enclosing schema document's `targetNamespace`.

##### <a id="composition-importLicenseReferences"></a>4.2.6.1 Licensing References to Components Across Namespaces

At least two conditions must be satisfied for a reference to be made to a foreign component: (1) there must be a means of addressing such foreign components, and (2) there must be a signal to schema-aware processors that a schema document contains such references. The namespace mechanisms defined by [[XML Namespaces 1.1]](#ref-xml-namespaces-1.1) satisfy the first requirement by allowing foreign components to be addressed. (How those components are located is governed by the processor's strategies for locating schema components in a given namespace, in which the `schemaLocation`attribute on the [<import>](#element-import) element can play a role; see also [Terminology of schema construction (§C.2)](#var_schema_con).)
